title: What is the event loop in JavaScript?
description: Explain how the JavaScript event loop works.
options: The event loop continuously checks the call stack, and when empty, moves callbacks from the task queue (macrotasks) or microtask queue to the stack for execution
correct_answer: The event loop continuously checks the call stack, and when empty, moves callbacks from the task queue (macrotasks) or microtask queue to the stack for execution

title: What is the difference between var, let, and const?
description: How do var, let, and const differ in scoping and reassignment?
options: var is function-scoped and hoisted (initialized as undefined); let and const are block-scoped and hoisted but not initialized (TDZ). const also cannot be reassigned
correct_answer: var is function-scoped and hoisted (initialized as undefined); let and const are block-scoped and hoisted but not initialized (TDZ). const also cannot be reassigned

title: What is closure?
description: What is a closure in JavaScript?
options: A function that retains access to its outer (lexical) scope's variables even after the outer function has returned
correct_answer: A function that retains access to its outer (lexical) scope's variables even after the outer function has returned

title: What is the prototype chain?
description: How does prototypal inheritance work?
options: Objects inherit properties through a chain of prototypes: when a property isn't found on an object, JavaScript looks up its __proto__ chain until it reaches null
correct_answer: Objects inherit properties through a chain of prototypes: when a property isn't found on an object, JavaScript looks up its __proto__ chain until it reaches null

title: What is the difference between Promise.all and Promise.allSettled?
description: How do they differ?
options: Promise.all rejects immediately if any promise rejects; Promise.allSettled waits for all to settle and returns {status, value/reason} for each
correct_answer: Promise.all rejects immediately if any promise rejects; Promise.allSettled waits for all to settle and returns {status, value/reason} for each

title: What is debouncing?
description: What is debouncing in JavaScript?
options: Delays function execution until after a specified time has elapsed since the last call; useful for search inputs, resize handlers
correct_answer: Delays function execution until after a specified time has elapsed since the last call; useful for search inputs, resize handlers

title: What is throttling?
description: How does throttling differ from debouncing?
options: Throttling ensures a function is called at most once per specified time interval; debouncing delays until activity stops
correct_answer: Throttling ensures a function is called at most once per specified time interval; debouncing delays until activity stops

title: What is the 'this' keyword?
description: How does 'this' work in different contexts?
options: In a regular function: depends on how it's called (global/object/new/call/apply/bind). Arrow functions: lexically inherit 'this' from enclosing scope
correct_answer: In a regular function: depends on how it's called (global/object/new/call/apply/bind). Arrow functions: lexically inherit 'this' from enclosing scope

title: What is destructuring?
description: What is object/array destructuring?
options: A syntax that extracts values from arrays/objects into distinct variables: const {name} = obj; const [a, b] = arr;
correct_answer: A syntax that extracts values from arrays/objects into distinct variables: const {name} = obj; const [a, b] = arr;

title: What is the spread operator?
description: What does ... (spread) do?
options: Expands iterables into individual elements: [...arr] for arrays, {...obj} for objects; used for copying, merging, and function arguments
correct_answer: Expands iterables into individual elements: [...arr] for arrays, {...obj} for objects; used for copying, merging, and function arguments

title: What is the rest parameter?
description: How does ...rest work in function parameters?
options: Collects remaining arguments into an array: function fn(a, ...rest); rest is a real array unlike the old arguments object
correct_answer: Collects remaining arguments into an array: function fn(a, ...rest); rest is a real array unlike the old arguments object

title: What is async/await?
description: How does async/await work?
options: async makes a function return a Promise; await pauses execution until the Promise resolves/rejects — syntactic sugar over .then() chains
correct_answer: async makes a function return a Promise; await pauses execution until the Promise resolves/rejects — syntactic sugar over .then() chains

title: What is the difference between == and ===?
description: How do loose and strict equality differ?
options: == performs type coercion before comparing; === compares both value and type without coercion. Always prefer ===
correct_answer: == performs type coercion before comparing; === compares both value and type without coercion. Always prefer ===

title: What are template literals?
description: What do backtick strings provide?
options: Multi-line strings, string interpolation with ${expression}, and tagged template support via backtick syntax
correct_answer: Multi-line strings, string interpolation with ${expression}, and tagged template support via backtick syntax

title: What is the module system in JavaScript?
description: How do ES modules work?
options: ES modules use import/export for static, tree-shakable module loading; CommonJS uses require/module.exports for Node.js sync loading
correct_answer: ES modules use import/export for static, tree-shakable module loading; CommonJS uses require/module.exports for Node.js sync loading

title: What is a WeakMap?
description: How does WeakMap differ from Map?
options: WeakMap keys must be objects and are weakly held (garbage-collectible); not iterable, no size property. Used for private data and preventing memory leaks
correct_answer: WeakMap keys must be objects and are weakly held (garbage-collectible); not iterable, no size property. Used for private data and preventing memory leaks

title: What is the Temporal API?
description: What will the Temporal API provide?
options: A modern date/time API (Stage 3 proposal) to replace Date, with proper timezone support, immutable objects, and clear distinction between absolute and local times
correct_answer: A modern date/time API (Stage 3 proposal) to replace Date, with proper timezone support, immutable objects, and clear distinction between absolute and local times

title: What is the Iterator protocol?
description: What makes an object iterable?
options: An object is iterable if it implements [Symbol.iterator]() returning an iterator with next() method that returns {value, done}
correct_answer: An object is iterable if it implements [Symbol.iterator]() returning an iterator with next() method that returns {value, done}

title: What is a tagged template literal?
description: How do tagged templates work?
options: A function called with template literal parts: tag`hello ${name}` invokes tag(strings, ...values) where strings is an array of string parts
correct_answer: A function called with template literal parts: tag`hello ${name}` invokes tag(strings, ...values) where strings is an array of string parts

title: What is the difference between Object.freeze and Object.seal?
description: How do they differ?
options: freeze: no property changes (add/remove/modify); seal: no add/remove but existing properties can be modified. Both are shallow
correct_answer: freeze: no property changes (add/remove/modify); seal: no add/remove but existing properties can be modified. Both are shallow

description: Create a basic Promise implementation with then and catch.

title: Implement curry function.
description: Write a function that curries another function.

title: Implement throttle function.
description: Write a throttle function.

title: Implement debounce function.
description: Write a debounce function.

title: Implement deep clone.
description: Write a deep clone function that handles nested objects, arrays, dates, and regex.

title: Implement Array.prototype.map polyfill.
description: Write a polyfill for Array.prototype.map.

title: Implement a pipe function.
description: Write a function that pipes functions from left to right.

title: Implement a simple EventEmitter.
description: Create an EventEmitter with on, off, emit, and once methods.

title: What is tree shaking?
description: What is tree shaking in JavaScript?
options: A dead code elimination technique used by bundlers to remove unused exports from the final bundle; relies on ES module static structure
correct_answer: A dead code elimination technique used by bundlers to remove unused exports from the final bundle; relies on ES module static structure

title: What is the difference between null and undefined?
description: How do null and undefined differ?
options: undefined means a variable is declared but not assigned; null is an intentional assignment of 'no value'. typeof undefined is 'undefined'; typeof null is 'object' (historical bug)
correct_answer: undefined means a variable is declared but not assigned; null is an intentional assignment of 'no value'. typeof undefined is 'undefined'; typeof null is 'object' (historical bug)

title: What is the difference between call, apply, and bind?
description: How do call, apply, and bind differ?
options: call invokes with individual args; apply invokes with array of args; bind returns a new function with bound 'this' without invoking. All set the this context
correct_answer: call invokes with individual args; apply invokes with array of args; bind returns a new function with bound 'this' without invoking. All set the this context

title: What is the difference between for...in and for...of?
description: How do they differ?
options: for...in iterates over enumerable property keys (strings, including inherited); for...of iterates over iterable values (arrays, maps, sets, strings)
correct_answer: for...in iterates over enumerable property keys (strings, including inherited); for...of iterates over iterable values (arrays, maps, sets, strings)

title: What is IIFE?
description: What is an Immediately Invoked Function Expression?
options: A function that is defined and executed immediately: (function() { })() — creates a private scope; less needed with ES6 block scoping
correct_answer: A function that is defined and executed immediately: (function() { })() — creates a private scope; less needed with ES6 block scoping

title: What is the difference between arrow functions and regular functions?
description: How do arrow functions differ?
options: Arrow functions: no own this/arguments/super, not hoisted, can't be constructors, concise syntax. Regular: own this, hoisted (declarations), can be constructors
correct_answer: Arrow functions: no own this/arguments/super, not hoisted, can't be constructors, concise syntax. Regular: own this, hoisted (declarations), can be constructors

title: What is a Service Worker?
description: What does a Service Worker do?
options: A script that runs in the background, separate from the web page; enables offline caching, push notifications, and background sync via Cache API and Fetch events
correct_answer: A script that runs in the background, separate from the web page; enables offline caching, push notifications, and background sync via Cache API and Fetch events

title: What is the Web Workers API?
description: What do Web Workers provide?
options: Run JavaScript in background threads; communicate with the main thread via postMessage; no direct DOM access — ideal for CPU-intensive tasks
correct_answer: Run JavaScript in background threads; communicate with the main thread via postMessage; no direct DOM access — ideal for CPU-intensive tasks

title: What is the difference between localStorage and sessionStorage?
description: How do they differ?
options: localStorage persists until explicitly cleared; sessionStorage is cleared when the tab/session ends. Both store ~5MB of string data per origin
correct_answer: localStorage persists until explicitly cleared; sessionStorage is cleared when the tab/session ends. Both store ~5MB of string data per origin

title: What are JavaScript modules?
description: What are the benefits of JavaScript modules?
options: Encapsulation, reusability, namespace management, dependency declaration, tree shaking support; each module has its own scope
correct_answer: Encapsulation, reusability, namespace management, dependency declaration, tree shaking support; each module has its own scope

title: What is JSON.stringify replacer?
description: What is the second argument to JSON.stringify?
options: A function or array that controls which properties are included in the output; function(key, value) can transform or filter values during serialization
correct_answer: A function or array that controls which properties are included in the output; function(key, value) can transform or filter values during serialization

title: Implement Promise.race.
description: Write a polyfill for Promise.race.

title: Implement memoize with TTL.
description: Create a memoize function with time-to-live cache expiration.

title: What is Object.create?
description: What does Object.create() do?
options: Creates a new object with the specified prototype and optional property descriptors; the cleanest way to set up prototypal inheritance
correct_answer: Creates a new object with the specified prototype and optional property descriptors; the cleanest way to set up prototypal inheritance
